# @hoge1e3/dom

Create HTML elements with a shorthand, JSX-like expression — without any transpilation or build step.

## Installation

```sh
npm install @hoge1e3/dom
```

## API

### `t: TagGenerator`

A pre-created, ready-to-use `TagGenerator` instance. Import and use directly.

### `tagGenerator(): TagGenerator`

Creates a new `TagGenerator` instance. Each property access on the generator (via `Proxy`) corresponds to an HTML tag name.

```typescript
export const t: TagGenerator;
export function tagGenerator(): TagGenerator;

type TagGenerator = {
  [key: string]: TagFunction;
};
type TagFunction = (...args: any[]) => HTMLElement;
type Attribute = object;
type SubElement = Node | string | number | boolean | null | undefined;
```

## Usage

```js
import { t } from "@hoge1e3/dom";

const el = t.div(
  { style: "color: blue;" },
  "Hello, ",
  t.button(
    {
      onclick() { alert("clicked!"); },
      style: "padding: 10px;",
    },
    "Click me"
  )
);
document.body.appendChild(el);
```

Produces:

```html
<div style="color: blue;">
  Hello, <button style="padding: 10px;">Click me</button>
</div>
```

with the `click` event listener already bound to the button.

## Attribute Rules

- Keys beginning with `on` (e.g. `onclick`, `oninput`) are treated as event listeners and registered via `addEventListener`.
- All other keys are set as attributes or properties on the element.
- Children (`SubElement`) can be `HTMLElement`, `string`, `number`, `boolean`, `null`, or `undefined`. Primitive values become text nodes; `null`/`undefined` are ignored.

## Notes

- Any tag name is accepted — `TagGenerator` uses a `Proxy` internally, so `t.myCustomElement(...)` works just as well as `t.div(...)`.
- Dependencies: `@hoge1e3/is-plain-object`, `@hoge1e3/dom-mutable-events`
